misconceptionSummary: Students confuse significant figures with decimal places — they think 1 s.f. always means one digit after the decimal point.

visuals.ruleText: A significant figure carries meaningful information about the size of a number
visuals.subText: Leading zeros just position the decimal point — they are not meaningful digits

steps.narration: Significant figures measure how precisely a number is known. A leading zero tells you only where the decimal point sits — it says nothing about the value's precision. Only digits that contribute to the size of the number count.

lines.text: Sig figs count from first non-zero digit
D.p. counts from the decimal point

steps.narration: The same number can have very different counts of significant figures and decimal places. Decimal places count from the decimal point; significant figures count from the first non-zero digit.

lines.text: Already 2 s.f. — trailing zeros hold place

steps.narration: For large integers, trailing zeros after rounding are placeholders — they hold the column positions but are not themselves significant. Forty-seven thousand to two significant figures is still forty-seven thousand.

visuals.label: Sig figs start at first non-zero digit — not at the decimal point

steps.narration: Zero point zero zero four six three. Three significant figures: four, six, three. The leading zeros remain as placeholders but are not counted.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: confusing 1 s.f. with 1 d.p. Decimal places count from the decimal point; significant figures count from the first non-zero digit — they are completely different measures.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Significant figures and decimal places are not the same thing. Sig figs count from the first non-zero digit; decimal places count from the decimal point.
